## Decision trees turn prediction into a sequence of splits

Decision trees are one of the clearest ways to explain supervised learning in
`TEM 0052`. The model keeps asking structured yes-or-no questions about the
features until it reaches a terminal node and produces a prediction.

That makes trees appealing for teaching because the prediction rule can be read as a
path rather than hidden inside one long equation. The same simplicity is also the
reason trees can become too eager to fit noise.

## How a tree grows

A tree starts with the full training sample at the root. It then searches for a
feature and threshold that improve the split according to a purity rule.

For classification, the usual split criteria are based on impurity, such as the Gini
index

$$
\mathrm{Gini}(S) = 1 - \sum_{c=1}^{C} p_c^2
$$

or entropy

$$
\mathrm{Entropy}(S) = -\sum_{c=1}^{C} p_c \log_2 p_c.
$$

For regression trees, the same logic uses reduction in squared error instead. The
important point for this course is not the exact algebra. It is that every split is
trying to create child nodes that are more homogeneous than the parent node.

## Why trees overfit so easily

A deep tree can keep splitting until it nearly memorizes the training sample. That
often looks impressive in-sample and unreliable out-of-sample.

This makes decision trees a strong illustration of the bias-variance trade-off:

- shallow trees usually have higher bias but lower variance,
- deep trees can capture local structure but may react strongly to noise,
- and pruning or depth limits are regularization choices, not cosmetic cleanup.

In practice, students should treat tree complexity as a tuning problem. `max_depth`,
`min_samples_split`, `min_samples_leaf`, and cost-complexity pruning all belong in
the same model-selection workflow as other hyperparameters.

::: {.teacher-only}
Ask students why an almost perfect training score from a large tree is usually a
warning sign rather than a success metric.
:::

## Where trees fit in `TEM 0052`

Decision trees sit between the early baseline models and the later ensemble methods.
They are more flexible than logistic regression, less geometry-driven than kNN, and
often easier to explain to a non-technical audience.

They also prepare students for two later ideas:

- cross-validation should choose the tree size, not classroom intuition,
- and single trees are often improved by bagging, random forests, or boosting.

That is why the model-assessment lab compares tree-based classification with logistic
regression and kNN under one shared evaluation rule.

## What to report

When students present a decision-tree workflow in `TEM 0052`, they should usually
report:

- the target definition and any preprocessing choices,
- the split criterion and the tuned complexity controls,
- the train/test or cross-validation protocol,
- whether the final tree is interpretable enough to inspect directly,
- and how the chosen tree compares with simpler baselines such as logistic regression
  or kNN.
